causes: Influenza viruses (Type A, B, C). Spreads through droplets when infected person coughs, sneezes, or talks.
precautions: Wash hands frequently with soap and water
Avoid touching eyes, nose, and mouth
Avoid close contact with sick people
home_remedies: Drink warm fluids like soup and herbal tea
Use honey and ginger tea for sore throat
emergency_signs: Difficulty breathing or shortness of breath
emergency_action: Call emergency services (112) immediately. Keep patient calm, ensure airway is clear, monitor breathing.
recovery_time: 5-7 days for most adults
advantages_of_treatment: Reduces severity and duration of illness
Prevents complications like pneumonia
Reduces risk of hospitalization
Protects vulnerable household members
diet_advice: Eat light, nutritious foods. Increase vitamin C intake. Stay well hydrated. Avoid alcohol and caffeine.
when_to_see_doctor: If symptoms worsen after 3 days, if fever exceeds 103°F (39.4°C), or if you are in a high-risk group (elderly, pregnant, immunocompromised).

causes: Insulin resistance and insufficient insulin production. Risk factors: obesity, sedentary lifestyle, family history, age over 45, prediabetes, high blood pressure.
precautions: Exercise at least 150 minutes per week
Avoid sugary drinks and processed foods
home_remedies: Fenugreek seeds soaked overnight in water
emergency_signs: Blood sugar below 70 mg/dL (hypoglycemia): shakiness, sweating, confusion
Blood sugar above 300 mg/dL (hyperglycemia): extreme thirst, fruity breath, vomiting
Diabetic ketoacidosis symptoms: nausea, abdominal pain, rapid breathing
emergency_action: For low blood sugar: Give 15g fast-acting carbs (glucose tablets, juice). For high blood sugar/unconsciousness: Call 112 immediately, do not give anything by mouth, monitor breathing.
recovery_time: Chronic condition requiring lifelong management
advantages_of_treatment: Prevents complications: kidney disease, blindness, nerve damage
Maintains normal blood sugar levels
Reduces risk of heart disease and stroke
Improves quality of life and energy levels
diet_advice: Low glycemic index foods, whole grains, leafy vegetables, lean proteins. Avoid white rice, white bread, sugary drinks, fried foods. Eat small frequent meals.
when_to_see_doctor: Immediately if newly diagnosed, if blood sugar is uncontrolled, or if you notice numbness, vision changes, or slow-healing wounds.

causes: Primary: unknown cause. Secondary: kidney disease, hormonal disorders, sleep apnea, certain medications. Risk factors: obesity, smoking, high sodium diet, stress, family history, age.
emergency_action: Call 112 immediately. Keep patient calm and still. Do NOT give extra medication without doctor instruction. Monitor vital signs. If unconscious, check airway and breathing.
recovery_time: Chronic condition managed with medication and lifestyle changes
advantages_of_treatment: Reduces risk of stroke by 35-40%
Reduces risk of heart attack by 20-25%
Prevents kidney damage
Protects blood vessel health
Reduces risk of heart failure
diet_advice: DASH diet: fruits, vegetables, whole grains, lean proteins, low-fat dairy. Reduce salt, processed foods, red meat, and sugary beverages.
when_to_see_doctor: If reading consistently above 130/80 mmHg, or immediately if above 180/120 mmHg.

symptoms: pain radiating to arm, jaw, neck, back
women may have atypical symptoms: nausea, jaw pain, back pain
causes: Blockage of coronary artery due to plaque rupture and blood clot. Risk factors: smoking, high blood pressure, high cholesterol, diabetes, obesity, family history.
precautions: Healthy diet low in saturated fats
home_remedies: EMERGENCY - No home treatment. Call 112 immediately.
While waiting for help: Chew aspirin 325mg if not allergic
medical_treatment: Beta-blockers, ACE inhibitors, statins for recovery
emergency_signs: Crushing chest pain lasting more than a few minutes
Pain spreading to shoulder, arm, back, neck, or jaw
Shortness of breath with or without chest pain
Breaking out in a cold sweat
emergency_action: CALL 112 IMMEDIATELY. Chew 325mg aspirin if available and not allergic. Sit or lie down in comfortable position. Do NOT drive yourself. Begin CPR if person is unresponsive and not breathing normally. Use AED if available.
recovery_time: Hospital: 3-7 days. Full recovery: 4-6 weeks for mild, 3-6 months for severe
advantages_of_treatment: Saves lives - faster treatment = less heart damage
Restores blood flow to heart muscle
Reduces risk of future heart attacks
Cardiac rehab improves long-term outcomes significantly
diet_advice: Heart-healthy diet: Mediterranean diet, omega-3 fatty acids, fruits, vegetables, whole grains. Avoid saturated fats, trans fats, excess sodium, processed meats.
when_to_see_doctor: ANY suspected heart attack is an emergency. Call 112 immediately. Do not wait to see if symptoms improve.

symptoms: difficulty sleeping due to breathing problems
coughing triggered by exercise, cold air, or allergens
causes: Airway inflammation and hypersensitivity. Triggers: allergens (dust, pollen, pet dander), respiratory infections, exercise, cold air, air pollution, smoke, certain medications.
precautions: Keep windows closed during high pollen season
home_remedies: Keep warm drinks handy during attacks
emergency_signs: Severe shortness of breath, can't speak in full sentences
Bluish tinge to lips or fingernails (cyanosis)
Rescue inhaler not working or wearing off quickly
Rapid breathing with neck muscle use
emergency_action: Call 112 immediately. Sit patient upright. Use rescue inhaler (4-10 puffs). Keep patient calm. Administer oxygen if available. Do NOT lay patient down.
recovery_time: Acute attack: minutes to hours with treatment. Chronic condition requiring ongoing management
advantages_of_treatment: Controls symptoms and improves quality of life
Prevents exercise limitations
Reduces emergency hospitalizations
Protects long-term lung function
diet_advice: Eat anti-inflammatory foods: fruits, vegetables, omega-3 fatty acids. Avoid sulfites (in wine, dried fruits), artificial preservatives. Maintain healthy weight.
when_to_see_doctor: At diagnosis, for medication adjustment, if rescue inhaler is used more than twice a week, or if symptoms disturb sleep.

causes: Dengue virus (DENV 1-4) transmitted by Aedes mosquitoes, especially Aedes aegypti.
precautions: Peak mosquito hours: dawn and dusk - stay protected
medical_treatment: Supportive care: IV fluids for dehydration
Paracetamol for fever (AVOID Aspirin and Ibuprofen - increase bleeding risk)
emergency_action: Call 112 or go to emergency immediately. Keep patient hydrated with oral rehydration solution. Monitor for bleeding. Do NOT give Aspirin or Ibuprofen. Lay patient down and keep comfortable.
recovery_time: 7-10 days. Severe dengue may require hospitalization
advantages_of_treatment: Early treatment prevents severe dengue hemorrhagic fever
IV fluids prevent shock
Regular monitoring catches complications early
Most patients recover completely with proper care
diet_advice: High fluid intake (ORS, coconut water, juices). Soft, easily digestible foods. Papaya, pomegranate, guava. Avoid oily, spicy foods. No alcohol.
when_to_see_doctor: At first suspicion of dengue (if in dengue-endemic area with fever), immediately if warning signs appear.

causes: Salmonella typhi bacteria. Spreads through contaminated food and water, fecal-oral route.
precautions: Drink only boiled or bottled water
Avoid street food in endemic areas
Wash hands before eating and after toilet
Avoid raw vegetables washed in tap water
home_remedies: Banana for easy digestion and electrolytes
Apple cider vinegar diluted in water
medical_treatment: Typhidot or Widal test for diagnosis
emergency_signs: Intestinal perforation (severe abdominal pain and rigidity)
High fever not responding to treatment
emergency_action: Call 112 immediately for complications. Keep patient in bed rest. Maintain hydration. Monitor temperature. Surgery required for intestinal perforation.
recovery_time: With antibiotics: 3-5 days improvement, 2 weeks full recovery. Without treatment: 3-4 weeks with significant mortality risk
advantages_of_treatment: Antibiotics reduce duration and severity dramatically
Prevents life-threatening complications
Reduces mortality from 15-20% to less than 1%
Vaccination provides 70-90% protection
diet_advice: Soft, easily digestible foods. Rice porridge, bananas, boiled vegetables. No spicy, oily, or raw foods. High calorie intake to combat weight loss. Plenty of fluids.
when_to_see_doctor: Immediately if fever lasts more than 3 days or if in area with typhoid history. Do not self-medicate antibiotics.

symptoms: cyclical fever with chills and sweating
fever cycles every 48-72 hours (depending on species)
causes: Plasmodium parasites (P. falciparum, P. vivax, P. ovale, P. malariae) transmitted by female Anopheles mosquitoes.
home_remedies: Citrus juices for hydration and vitamin C
medical_treatment: Blood smear and RDT for diagnosis
emergency_signs: High fever above 40°C unresponsive to treatment
emergency_action: Call 112 immediately. Keep patient horizontal if unconscious. Manage seizures (do not restrain). Cool the patient with damp cloth for high fever. Administer IV fluids if available.
recovery_time: Uncomplicated malaria: 2-7 days with treatment. Severe malaria: 1-4 weeks hospitalization
advantages_of_treatment: Rapid parasite clearance
Prevention of severe malaria complications
Complete cure possible with appropriate treatment
Prevents relapse with primaquine
diet_advice: High carbohydrate foods for energy. Fresh fruits and vegetables. Avoid spicy and fatty foods. Plenty of fluids. Iron-rich foods for anemia recovery.
when_to_see_doctor: Immediately if in malaria-endemic area and experiencing fever, chills, and sweating. Do not delay — P. falciparum can be fatal within 24-48 hours.

symptoms: chest pain when breathing or coughing
causes: Mycobacterium tuberculosis bacteria. Spreads through air when infected person coughs, sneezes, or speaks. Latent TB can become active when immunity weakens.
precautions: Adequate ventilation in homes and workplaces
Avoid close contact with active TB patients
Complete full course of TB treatment
medical_treatment: First-line drugs: Isoniazid, Rifampicin, Pyrazinamide, Ethambutol
Drug-resistant TB: 18-24 months with second-line drugs
Regular sputum tests to monitor progress
emergency_signs: Massive hemoptysis (coughing large amounts of blood)
TB meningitis (severe headache, stiff neck, confusion)
emergency_action: Call 112 immediately. Position patient with affected side down for hemoptysis. Keep airway clear. Do not give cough suppressants if coughing blood. Hospital admission required.
recovery_time: Active TB: 6-9 months of treatment. Multidrug-resistant TB: 18-24 months
advantages_of_treatment: 95%+ cure rate with proper DOTS therapy
Eliminates infectiousness within 2-3 weeks of treatment
Prevents drug resistance development
Free treatment available in India under RNTCP
diet_advice: High protein diet (eggs, meat, legumes), high calorie intake, vitamin D and calcium-rich foods, fresh fruits and vegetables. Avoid alcohol.
when_to_see_doctor: If cough persists more than 2-3 weeks, if coughing blood, unexplained weight loss, or if you have been in contact with a TB patient.

symptoms: chest pain when breathing or coughing
causes: Bacterial (Streptococcus pneumoniae most common), viral (influenza, COVID-19), fungal, or aspiration. Risk factors: age extremes, smoking, chronic diseases, hospitalization.
precautions: Avoid close contact with sick individuals
medical_treatment: Antibiotics for bacterial pneumonia: Amoxicillin, Azithromycin, Doxycycline
IV antibiotics and fluids for hospital care
emergency_action: Call 112 immediately. Position patient upright at 45 degrees to ease breathing. Administer supplemental oxygen if available. Monitor breathing continuously.
recovery_time: Mild cases: 1-3 weeks. Severe cases: 4-6 weeks or longer
advantages_of_treatment: Early antibiotic treatment dramatically improves outcomes
Oxygen therapy prevents hypoxia complications
Hospitalization mortality significantly reduced with proper care
Vaccination prevents most cases
diet_advice: High protein and vitamin C-rich foods. Warm fluids and soups. Fruits and vegetables. Easy to digest foods. Avoid dairy if it thickens mucus.
when_to_see_doctor: Immediately if breathing is difficult, if fever is high and persistent, or if symptoms worsen after initial improvement.

symptoms: new loss of taste or smell
causes: SARS-CoV-2 coronavirus. Spreads primarily through respiratory droplets and aerosols.
precautions: Wear masks in crowded indoor spaces
home_remedies: Kadha (herbal decoction with tulsi, ginger, black pepper)
Vitamin C, D, and Zinc supplements
medical_treatment: Dexamethasone for severe cases requiring oxygen
Oxygen therapy and mechanical ventilation for severe cases
emergency_signs: Confusion or inability to stay awake
emergency_action: Call 112 immediately. Prone positioning (lying face down) can improve oxygen levels while waiting for help. Monitor SpO2 with pulse oximeter. Administer supplemental oxygen if available.
recovery_time: Mild cases: 1-2 weeks. Severe cases: several weeks. Long COVID can persist months.
advantages_of_treatment: Vaccination reduces severe disease by 90%+
Early antiviral treatment reduces hospitalization
Oxygen therapy is life-saving in severe cases
Reduces long COVID risk
diet_advice: Immunity-boosting foods: citrus fruits, ginger, turmeric, garlic, vegetables. Adequate protein. Zinc-rich foods. Stay well hydrated.
when_to_see_doctor: Immediately if breathlessness, SpO2 drops below 95%, or symptoms are rapidly worsening.

symptoms: severe pain in back and side (renal colic)
pain radiating to lower abdomen and groin
fever and chills if infection present
causes: Calcium oxalate (most common), uric acid, struvite, or cystine stones. Risk factors: dehydration, high oxalate/sodium/protein diet, family history, obesity.
precautions: Reduce oxalate-rich foods (spinach, nuts) if susceptible
Avoid vitamin C supplements in excess
medical_treatment: Small stones: pain management + fluid intake
Stone dissolution therapy for uric acid stones
emergency_signs: Excruciating pain not relieved by position change
Fever and chills with stone (indicates infection)
emergency_action: Call 112 if fever with severe pain (kidney infection/obstruction emergency). Administer IV pain relief (ketorolac, morphine) in ER. Hydration and antispasmodics. Emergency urological intervention if blockage.
recovery_time: Small stones: days to weeks to pass. Surgery: 1-2 weeks recovery
advantages_of_treatment: Pain relief is rapid with medication
ESWL is non-invasive with high success rate
Stone analysis guides prevention of future stones
Complete stone clearance prevents future kidney damage
diet_advice: High water intake. Reduce salt and animal protein. For calcium oxalate stones: limit spinach, nuts, chocolate. Citrus fruits helpful. No excess vitamin supplements.
when_to_see_doctor: If passing blood in urine, if pain is severe, or if fever develops with urinary symptoms.

symptoms: pain starting around navel and shifting to lower right abdomen
pain worsens with movement, coughing, or breathing
causes: Blockage of appendix lumen (fecalith, lymphoid hyperplasia, parasite). Leads to infection, inflammation, and potential rupture.
precautions: Seek immediate medical attention for abdominal pain
home_remedies: EMERGENCY - No home treatment. Go to emergency immediately.
Do NOT apply heat to abdomen
Do NOT give laxatives or enemas
Do NOT eat or drink (NPO status for potential surgery)
medical_treatment: IV antibiotics before and after surgery
Conservative antibiotics for uncomplicated cases in some patients
Drainage of abscess if ruptured appendix with abscess
emergency_signs: ANY persistent abdominal pain lasting more than 4-6 hours
Pain that moves from navel to lower right abdomen
Signs of rupture: sudden temporary pain relief followed by spreading pain
emergency_action: GO TO EMERGENCY ROOM IMMEDIATELY. Do not eat or drink. Do not take laxatives. Keep patient comfortable. Surgery is required - early treatment prevents rupture. Call 112.
recovery_time: Laparoscopic surgery: 1-2 weeks. Open surgery/ruptured: 4-6 weeks
advantages_of_treatment: Early surgery before rupture has near-100% success
Laparoscopic surgery allows quick recovery
Antibiotics reduce post-surgical complications
Prevents life-threatening peritonitis
diet_advice: Post-surgery: clear liquids initially, progressing to normal diet over 1-2 weeks. High fiber diet after recovery.
when_to_see_doctor: IMMEDIATELY - do not wait. Appendicitis is a surgical emergency. Delay can lead to rupture and life-threatening peritonitis.

symptoms: severe throbbing or pulsating headache (usually one side)
aura: visual disturbances, tingling, speech problems (in some people)
causes: Neurological disorder with genetic and environmental factors. Triggers: stress, hormonal changes, certain foods (cheese, wine, chocolate), bright lights, strong smells, sleep disruption.
precautions: Keep a migraine diary to identify triggers
home_remedies: Ice pack or cold compress on forehead
Caffeine (small amount can help some migraines)
medical_treatment: Preventive: Beta-blockers, Topiramate, Amitriptyline, CGRP antagonists
emergency_signs: Worst headache of your life (thunderclap headache)
Headache with fever, stiff neck, confusion (meningitis sign)
Sudden severe headache with vision changes or weakness
emergency_action: Call 112 immediately for thunderclap headache or headache with neurological symptoms. This may indicate subarachnoid hemorrhage. Keep patient calm, note time of onset.
recovery_time: Individual attack: 4-72 hours. Chronic condition requiring ongoing management
advantages_of_treatment: Triptans abort attack in 80% of cases
Preventive medications reduce attack frequency by 50%
Early treatment during aura phase most effective
Lifestyle modifications can significantly reduce frequency
diet_advice: Avoid known triggers: aged cheeses, processed meats, alcohol (especially red wine), MSG, artificial sweeteners. Regular meals. Adequate hydration. Magnesium-rich foods.
when_to_see_doctor: If migraines are frequent (4+ per month), if over-the-counter medication no longer helps, or if headache pattern changes suddenly.

symptoms: persistent sad, empty, or hopeless mood
loss of interest in activities once enjoyed
changes in appetite (weight loss or gain)
causes: Combination of genetic, biological, environmental, and psychological factors. Brain chemistry imbalances, life events, trauma, chronic illness can trigger depression.
precautions: Seek help early at first signs
home_remedies: St. John's Wort (mild depression only - check drug interactions)
emergency_signs: Complete inability to care for oneself
emergency_action: If person is in immediate danger of suicide: Call iCall (9152987821) or Vandrevala Foundation (1860-2662-345) or 112. Do not leave person alone. Remove access to means. Stay calm and listen. Get immediate professional help.
recovery_time: Medication: 4-6 weeks to feel effect. Full recovery: months to years with proper treatment
advantages_of_treatment: 80% of people respond well to treatment
Restores quality of life and functioning
Prevents suicide risk
Combination therapy has best long-term outcomes
diet_advice: Mediterranean diet associated with lower depression risk. Omega-3 rich foods. B vitamins (leafy greens, whole grains). Avoid excessive alcohol and sugar.
when_to_see_doctor: If symptoms last more than 2 weeks, if they interfere with daily functioning, or if any thoughts of self-harm are present.

symptoms: sudden numbness or weakness in face, arm, or leg (especially one side)
sudden confusion, trouble speaking or understanding
sudden vision problems in one or both eyes
sudden severe headache with no known cause
sudden trouble walking, dizziness, loss of balance
drooping of face on one side
causes: Ischemic stroke (clot blocking blood flow) or hemorrhagic stroke (bleeding in brain). Risk factors: hypertension, diabetes, atrial fibrillation, smoking, high cholesterol, obesity.
home_remedies: EMERGENCY - No home treatment. Time is brain. Call 112 immediately.
Do not give food, water, or medication
medical_treatment: Ischemic: tPA (clot buster) within 4.5 hours of onset
emergency_signs: Use FAST: Face drooping, Arm weakness, Speech difficulty, Time to call 112
emergency_action: CALL 112 IMMEDIATELY. Note exact time of symptom onset (critical for treatment eligibility). Keep patient lying down with head slightly elevated. Do NOT give aspirin (may worsen hemorrhagic stroke). Do not give food/water.
recovery_time: Varies widely: days to months to years of rehabilitation. Early treatment dramatically improves outcomes.
advantages_of_treatment: tPA within 3 hours reduces disability by 30%
Thrombectomy can restore function in large vessel occlusion
Every 30-minute delay means additional brain damage
Rehabilitation can recover significant function over time
diet_advice: Post-stroke: Mediterranean diet, low sodium, high potassium. Dysphagia diet if swallowing affected. Adequate protein for recovery.
when_to_see_doctor: IMMEDIATELY - Stroke is a time-critical emergency. 'Time is brain' - faster treatment = less brain damage.

symptoms: pale skin and pallor of eyelids/gums
causes: Insufficient red blood cells or hemoglobin. Most common: iron deficiency, B12/folate deficiency, chronic disease, blood loss (menstruation, GI bleed), hemolysis.
precautions: Vitamin C with iron-rich foods enhances absorption
Treat underlying causes of blood loss
Avoid tea/coffee with meals (reduces iron absorption)
home_remedies: Eat spinach, lentils, beans, tofu, fortified cereals
medical_treatment: Iron supplements: Ferrous sulfate, Ferrous fumarate
IV iron infusion for malabsorption or intolerance
emergency_signs: Severe shortness of breath at rest
emergency_action: Call 112 for hemoglobin crisis or active hemorrhage. Blood transfusion may be needed. Maintain airway and oxygen. Monitor vital signs.
recovery_time: Iron levels improve in 2-3 weeks. Full recovery: 3-6 months of supplementation
advantages_of_treatment: Rapid improvement in energy and cognitive function
Prevention of pregnancy complications with folate
Restoration of normal hemoglobin prevents organ damage
Treatment of cause prevents recurrence
diet_advice: Increase: red meat, poultry, fish, lentils, spinach, fortified foods. Eat vitamin C with iron-rich foods. Avoid: tea, coffee, calcium with iron-rich meals.
when_to_see_doctor: If fatigue is persistent, if you notice pallor, or if known to have risk factors for anemia (heavy periods, pregnancy, vegetarian diet).

symptoms: yellowing of skin and eyes (scleral icterus)
causes: Pre-hepatic: hemolysis. Hepatic: viral hepatitis (A, B, C), alcoholic liver disease, cirrhosis. Post-hepatic: gallstones, pancreatic cancer, bile duct obstruction.
emergency_action: Call 112 for acute liver failure symptoms. Restrict protein intake temporarily. No alcohol or hepatotoxic drugs. Monitor mental status closely. Hospital admission urgently needed.
recovery_time: Hepatitis A: 2-6 weeks. Obstructive jaundice: resolves with treatment. Liver disease: months to chronic
advantages_of_treatment: Early treatment prevents liver failure
Antiviral treatment can cure hepatitis C
ERCP relieves obstruction non-surgically
Liver transplant curative for end-stage liver disease
diet_advice: High carbohydrate, low fat diet. Avoid alcohol completely. Papaya, sugarcane juice, lemon water. No spicy or oily foods.
when_to_see_doctor: Immediately when yellow discoloration first noticed. Any jaundice requires medical evaluation.

causes: Autoimmune disorder where immune system attacks joint lining (synovium). Genetic factors + environmental triggers. More common in women.
precautions: Early aggressive treatment prevents joint damage
medical_treatment: Joint replacement surgery in advanced cases
emergency_signs: Joint sepsis (septic arthritis): fever with hot, red, swollen joint
Pericarditis or pleuritis with chest pain
Cervical spine instability with neurological symptoms
emergency_action: Call 112 for septic arthritis (extremely painful, hot, swollen joint with fever). Do not manipulate the joint. IV antibiotics and joint aspiration urgently needed.
recovery_time: Chronic condition. Treatment can achieve remission. Joint damage is cumulative without proper treatment.
advantages_of_treatment: Early treatment prevents permanent joint damage
Biologics achieve remission in 40-60% of patients
Maintains function and quality of life
Reduces cardiovascular complications
diet_advice: Anti-inflammatory diet: fatty fish, olive oil, fruits, vegetables, nuts. Mediterranean diet. Avoid processed foods, sugar, red meat. Omega-3 supplements.
when_to_see_doctor: As soon as joint symptoms appear. Early treatment (within 3-6 months of onset) significantly improves outcomes.

symptoms: itchy rash with blisters (starts on chest, back, face)
blisters progress from clear to cloudy then crust over
causes: Varicella-zoster virus (VZV). Highly contagious through air droplets and contact with blisters. Contagious 1-2 days before rash until blisters crust.
precautions: Keep children home until all blisters crust
Avoid contact with vulnerable people (pregnant, immunocompromised)
home_remedies: Keep nails trimmed to prevent scratching
medical_treatment: Antiviral: Acyclovir (within 24 hours of rash for high-risk)
Paracetamol for fever (NOT aspirin - Reye's syndrome risk)
Antibiotics only if secondary bacterial infection
emergency_signs: Bacterial skin infection (increased redness, warmth, pus)
Fever above 103°F lasting more than 3-4 days
emergency_action: Call 112 if breathing difficulty, seizures, or confusion occurs. Antiviral therapy urgently needed for complications. Never give aspirin to children with chickenpox.
recovery_time: 1-2 weeks. Blisters crust over within 1 week.
advantages_of_treatment: Antiviral reduces duration and severity significantly
Vaccine prevents infection in 95% and reduces severity
Calamine lotion provides significant symptomatic relief
Complete immunity after infection
diet_advice: Soft, cold foods if mouth blisters present. Cold yogurt, smoothies. Adequate fluids. Avoid acidic foods. Vitamin C-rich foods.
when_to_see_doctor: If rash affects eyes, if there are signs of secondary infection, if breathing is affected, or if patient is immunocompromised, newborn, or adult (higher complication risk).

symptoms: burning or gnawing stomach pain (between meals or at night)
pain improves with eating or antacids (duodenal ulcer)
causes: H. pylori bacterial infection (most common), NSAID overuse. Risk factors: stress, smoking, alcohol, spicy food.
precautions: Avoid NSAIDs (Aspirin, Ibuprofen) or take with food/PPI
Test and treat H. pylori if present
medical_treatment: H. pylori eradication: Triple therapy (PPI + Amoxicillin + Clarithromycin)
Endoscopy for diagnosis and bleeding management
emergency_signs: Signs of shock (pale, rapid pulse, low BP)
emergency_action: Call 112 immediately for bleeding ulcer or perforation. Do not eat or drink. Keep patient still. IV fluids urgently needed. Emergency endoscopy or surgery may be required.
recovery_time: H. pylori eradication: 2 weeks. Ulcer healing: 4-8 weeks of PPI therapy
advantages_of_treatment: H. pylori eradication cures >90% of ulcers
PPIs dramatically accelerate healing
Treatment prevents life-threatening complications
Endoscopy can stop bleeding without surgery in many cases
diet_advice: Bland diet: oatmeal, bananas, rice, applesauce. Probiotic foods. Avoid spicy, acidic, caffeinated foods. Eat small frequent meals. No alcohol.
when_to_see_doctor: If burning stomach pain persists, if taking NSAIDs regularly, or if any black stools or blood in vomit are noted.

symptoms: frequent urge to urinate with small amounts
causes: Most common: E. coli bacteria (80%). Other bacteria: Klebsiella, Proteus. Risk factors: female anatomy, sexual activity, catheter use, diabetes, menopause.
medical_treatment: Urine culture to guide antibiotic choice
Recurrent UTIs: prophylactic antibiotics or post-coital antibiotics
emergency_signs: High fever above 103°F with UTI symptoms (kidney infection/sepsis)
Nausea and vomiting preventing oral medication
emergency_action: Call 112 if high fever with UTI symptoms (urosepsis risk). IV antibiotics needed urgently. Hospital admission for urosepsis. Adequate IV fluid resuscitation.
recovery_time: Lower UTI: 3-7 days. Kidney infection: 7-14 days
advantages_of_treatment: Rapid symptom relief within 24-48 hours
Prevents spread to kidneys and bloodstream
Urine culture ensures correct antibiotic
Recurrence prevention strategies effective
diet_advice: High water intake. Cranberry juice or supplements. Probiotic foods. Avoid sugary drinks, caffeine, alcohol. Vitamin C-rich foods to acidify urine.
when_to_see_doctor: At first symptoms, especially if pregnant, if symptoms don't improve in 2-3 days, or if fever/back pain develops.
